structured_choices.data_source: 10,000 patient feedback responses collected via clinic app over 24 months (Jan Year 1 – Dec Year 2). Each response includes: timestamped text feedback, structured demographics (age, treatment type, duration in care, prior adherence history), and binary adherence label derived from clinical follow-up outcomes (medication refill records, appointment attendance, clinician assessment) within 90 days post-feedback.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary adherence label: 1 (adherent) if patient demonstrates ≥80% medication adherence by refill records AND attends ≥75% of scheduled appointments within 90 days post-feedback; 0 (non-adherent) otherwise. Label assigned blind to feedback sentiment to prevent circularity. Adherence outcome measured independently of feedback content.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified temporal split to prevent label leakage and respect temporal dynamics of sentiment. Split by time period AND patient cohort: (1) Training: months 1–12, all patients. (2) Validation: months 13–18, all patients. (3) Test: months 19–24, held-out cohort of patients with no prior appearances in train/val. Within each temporal window, stratify by (treatment_type, baseline_adherence_risk_category) to ensure representative class balance and demographic coverage across splits. This approach prevents data leakage from future adherence outcomes influencing training, accounts for sentiment drift over time, and ensures test performance reflects generalization to new patient populations seen in later months.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (text tokenizer, vocabulary, scaling for demographics) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–12). Validation and test sets are transformed using the training-fitted vocabulary and scaling parameters without refitting. This prevents information from validation/test leakage into preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: Input features: (1) Text features: patient feedback text as sequences of tokens (vocabulary size 5,000, maximum sequence length 200 tokens, rare words mapped to <UNK>). (2) Contextual metadata: (a) patient tenure in clinic (days), (b) treatment type (categorical, one-hot encoded), (c) prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average, fit on train data only), (d) time-since-last-appointment (days), (e) feedback submission time-of-day (categorical: morning, afternoon, evening). Metadata features are scaled (standardization) using training set statistics. No engineered features derived from the label or future adherence outcomes.
structured_choices.model: Bidirectional LSTM with attention mechanism. Architecture: embedding layer (300 dimensions, fit on training vocab), BiLSTM (128 hidden units, 2 layers with dropout=0.3), attention layer, dense layer (64 units, ReLU), output sigmoid. Trained with binary cross-entropy loss, Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.001), batch size 32, maximum 50 epochs with early stopping on validation loss (patience=5 epochs). Hyperparameters (embedding_dim, hidden_units, dropout, attention_mechanism yes/no) selected via grid search on validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Lexicon-based sentiment analysis (VADER sentiment analyzer applied to feedback text, yielding compound sentiment score ∈ [−1, 1]) combined with logistic regression on the same metadata features (tenure, treatment type, prior adherence rate, time-since-last-appointment, submission time-of-day). Sentiment score + metadata input to logistic regression trained on training set. Logistic regression hyperparameter (regularization strength C) tuned via grid search on validation set using identical train/val split. This baseline is fair: it uses the same training data, the same metadata, identical tuning methodology (grid search on validation), and is evaluated on the same test set under identical conditions. The comparison isolates the contribution of RNN-based learned representations versus fixed lexicon-based sentiment.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC-AUC). Justification: (1) Stakeholders prioritize reducing dropout/non-adherence; ROC-AUC is threshold-agnostic and captures the model's ranking ability across decision thresholds, enabling clinicians to choose a threshold that balances sensitivity (catching non-adherent patients for intervention) and specificity (avoiding over-intervention on adherent patients). (2) The data is imbalanced (adherence base rates often 60–70% in mental health); ROC-AUC is not sensitive to class imbalance, whereas accuracy would be misleading. (3) Operationally, clinicians will set a decision threshold based on resource constraints for interventions, so the full ROC curve is more informative than a single point metric.
secondary_metrics: Precision at recall ≥0.75 (how many flagged non-adherent patients are truly non-adherent when the model recalls ≥75% of non-adherent cases; important for targeting limited intervention resources)
F1-score (harmonic mean of precision and recall; secondary check for balanced performance)

Calibration: Brier score and calibration plot (predicted probability vs. observed frequency); important for ensuring model confidence aligns with true adherence outcomes when clinicians use predicted probabilities to prioritize interventions
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning is performed using the validation set (months 13–18). A grid search (or random search) explores RNN hyperparameters (embedding dimension ∈ {100, 200, 300}, hidden units ∈ {64, 128, 256}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.3, 0.5}, attention yes/no) and baseline logistic regression C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}. For each hyperparameter set, the model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set using ROC-AUC. The hyperparameter set with highest validation ROC-AUC is selected. The test set is not used for model selection and is touched only once, at the end, for final evaluation.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set (months 19–24, held-out cohort) is used exactly once, after model selection is complete. Both the RNN and baseline logistic regression are retrained on the combined training + validation data (months 1–18) using the selected hyperparameters, then evaluated on the held-out test set. This maximizes training data while preventing test leakage. Results are reported: ROC-AUC, secondary metrics, and 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap or binomial proportion).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Known confound: External events (protocol changes, patient engagement initiatives) may affect adherence rates independently of sentiment. Control approach: (1) Stratify test set by time period and check whether both RNN and baseline maintain consistent performance across early vs. late test months; if performance degrades in late months, this signals external confounding. (2) Extract temporal features (months since study start) as model inputs; examine whether the model learns to rely on these as proxies for external events. (3) Post-hoc analysis: segment test set by documented protocol changes (if available from clinic records) and report separate test metrics for each segment; if performance differs sharply, external confounding is suspected. (4) In deployment, continuously monitor performance on a held-out recent cohort (e.g., monthly) to detect drift from external interventions. The experiment tests the hypothesis in the observed data but does not isolate causal effects of sentiment; external confounding is a threat to internal validity and is flagged rather than eliminated by design.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN-Based Sentiment Analysis for Treatment Adherence Prediction

### 1. Data Source and Label Definition

The analysis will use 10,000 patient feedback responses collected via the clinic app over 24 months (January Year 1 through December Year 2). Each response includes timestamped text feedback, structured demographic information (age, treatment type, time in care, prior adherence history), and a binary adherence outcome derived from clinical records.

The adherence label is defined as follows: a patient is marked adherent (label = 1) if they demonstrate ≥80% medication adherence by pharmacy refill records AND attend ≥75% of scheduled appointments within 90 days following the feedback submission. All other patients are marked non-adherent (label = 0). Critically, the label is assigned using clinical data independent of the feedback text, preventing circular reasoning in which the sentiment itself determines the label.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

To prevent label leakage and capture temporal dynamics of patient sentiment, we employ a stratified temporal split:

- **Training set:** Months 1–12 (all patients)
- **Validation set:** Months 13–18 (all patients)
- **Test set:** Months 19–24 (held-out cohort of patients with no prior feedback in training or validation)

Within each time window, data is stratified by (treatment_type, baseline_adherence_risk_category) to ensure class balance and demographic representation across splits. This temporal structure prevents information leakage from future adherence outcomes into the training window, respects the sequential nature of patient feedback, and ensures that the test set represents truly unseen patients encountered in later months—a realistic deployment scenario.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Text features:** Patient feedback is tokenized into sequences (vocabulary size 5,000, max length 200 tokens). Rare words are mapped to a special token. The tokenizer is fit exclusively on the training set.

**Metadata features:** We retain (1) patient tenure in clinic (days), (2) treatment type (one-hot encoded), (3) prior adherence rate (rolling 6-month average), (4) time since last appointment (days), and (5) feedback submission time-of-day. All continuous metadata are standardized using mean and standard deviation computed from the training set only; validation and test data are transformed using these training statistics.

**Preprocessing fit point:** All transformers (tokenizer, scaling statistics) are fit on the training set only and then applied to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents data leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Primary model:** A bidirectional LSTM with attention mechanism. The architecture consists of an embedding layer (300 dimensions), two BiLSTM layers (128 hidden units each, dropout 0.3), an attention layer, a dense layer (64 units, ReLU), and a sigmoid output. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.001) for up to 50 epochs with early stopping on validation loss.

**Baseline:** Lexicon-based sentiment analysis using the VADER sentiment analyzer, which produces a compound sentiment score for each piece of feedback. This score is concatenated with the same metadata features and fed into a logistic regression classifier trained on the training set. The baseline uses the same data, the same metadata, and the same tuning methodology (grid search on the validation set) as the primary model. This ensures a fair comparison that isolates the contribution of learned RNN representations over fixed lexicon-based sentiment.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC-AUC). Justification: ROC-AUC is threshold-agnostic and captures ranking ability across decision boundaries—critical because clinicians will choose thresholds based on intervention resource availability. ROC-AUC is robust to class imbalance (common in mental health adherence data) and operationally relevant for prioritizing patients for intervention.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision at recall ≥0.75 (fraction of flagged non-adherent patients who are truly non-adherent when the model captures ≥75% of true non-adherent cases)
- F1-score (harmonic mean of precision and recall)
- Calibration metrics: Brier score and calibration plots (to ensure predicted probabilities align with observed outcomes, enabling clinicians to trust model confidence scores for intervention prioritization)

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed via grid search on the validation set (months 13–18). We explore: embedding dimension ∈ {100, 200, 300}, hidden units ∈ {64, 128, 256}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.3, 0.5}, and attention (yes/no). For the baseline, we tune logistic regression regularization C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}. The hyperparameter set maximizing validation ROC-AUC is selected. The test set is not touched during this phase.

### 7. Final Evaluation

Once hyperparameters are selected, both the RNN and baseline are retrained on the combined training and validation data (months 1–18) using the selected hyperparameters, then evaluated on the held-out test set (months 19–24). Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals. The test set is used exactly once.

### 8. Confound Controls

External events (protocol changes, engagement initiatives) may affect adherence independently of sentiment. We address this threat by: (1) stratifying test-set results by early vs. late months to detect performance drift; (2) including temporal features in model inputs and examining whether the model relies on these as proxies for external events; (3) performing post-hoc analysis on documented protocol changes from clinic records; and (4) recommending continuous monitoring in deployment. The experiment does not causally isolate sentiment from external confounds but flags this as a threat to internal validity.
